perturbation.original_text: 1.1 Selling Shareholder agrees, during the period ending five years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3:
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 Selling Shareholder agrees to reasonably attempt, during the period ending five years from the Effective Time ("Restricted Period"), that neither Selling Shareholder nor any affiliate of Selling Shareholder shall conduct any of the following activities described in Sections 1.1.1, 1.1.2, and 1.1.3:
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'reasonably attempt,' the obligation becomes vague. The original clause strictly prohibits certain activities, while the modified clause only requires a reasonable attempt to avoid them. This introduces ambiguity regarding the level of effort required for compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The modified text, by using 'reasonably attempt,' creates a potential loophole. While still aiming to restrict certain activities, the lack of a strict prohibition could be interpreted as an insufficient restraint, thus potentially violating Section 16600, which favors open competition and voids agreements that restrain trade, as the 'reasonable attempt' may be interpreted as an invalid restraint of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)

(b)(1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937 , to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. perturbation.justification: The addition of "reasonably attempt" significantly weakens the original obligation, potentially contradicting California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The original text imposed a strict prohibition, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity. The law snippet states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By adding 'reasonably attempt,' the restraint is no longer absolute, creating a loophole that may be deemed an insufficient restraint of trade, thereby conflicting with the statute's intent as highlighted in both scraped law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: 1.  NON-COMPETITION COVENANT. The Selling Shareholder acknowledges (i) as an [director,] officer and principal shareholder of SAI, he has been instrumental in the Business of SAI and its success and (ii) has been privy to and had access to the offices, personnel, technology, confidential and proprietary information of SAI.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  NON-COMPETITION COVENANT. The Selling Shareholder acknowledges that he was somehow involved in the Business of SAI and its success, and he may have had some access to the offices, personnel, technology, confidential and proprietary information of SAI.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the Selling Shareholder has been instrumental and has had access. The modified text uses weaker language, such as 'somehow involved' and 'may have had some access.' This creates ambiguity about the actual extent of the shareholder's involvement and access, making it more difficult to prove they possess confidential information that warrants a non-compete agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: In California, non-compete agreements are generally void except in limited circumstances, such as the sale of a business where the seller possesses trade secrets or confidential information. By casting doubt on the Selling Shareholder's access to confidential information, the modified text weakens the justification for the non-compete agreement. The vagueness of the modified text can cause issues when attempting to defend the non-compete's enforcement, potentially conflicting with the need for clarity under California law regarding the justification for restricting someone's ability to work, potentially impacting public policy considerations related to fair competition and employee mobility, in line with Cal. Code Regs. section 5630 which involves definitions and requirements relevant to employment and business operations in CA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Message - California Code of Regulations This document is not available on Westlaw.
perturbation.justification: The change in text from 'instrumental in the Business of SAI and its success' to 'somehow involved' and from 'has been privy to and had access to the offices, personnel, technology, confidential and proprietary information of SAI' to 'may have had some access' significantly weakens the assertion that the Selling Shareholder possesses confidential information. This undermines the legal basis for a non-compete agreement, especially in jurisdictions like California where such agreements are heavily scrutinized, as the Law Explanation suggests. The Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition states a contradiction occurs when an obligation is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder shall not, without the prior written approval of Dollar Tree, at any time copy, reproduce, disclose or divulge to any third party, use or permit others to use any confidential information of SAI or Dollar Tree (including financial information, sources of supplies and materials, prospective and existing expansion programs, joint ventures and affiliate agreements, business systems and marketing methods, and all business affairs).
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder should attempt to not, without what they consider reasonable prior consultation with Dollar Tree, copy, reproduce, disclose or divulge to any third party, use or permit others to use what they reasonably consider confidential information of SAI or Dollar Tree.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces several ambiguities. Replacing 'shall not' with 'should attempt to not' weakens the prohibition. 'Reasonable prior consultation' lacks a clear definition, as does 'what they reasonably consider confidential information.' This subjectivity undermines the clarity of the original obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines trade secrets and provides legal remedies for misappropriation. By making the definition of 'confidential information' subjective ('what they reasonably consider'), the modified text creates uncertainty about what information is protected. This ambiguity could make it difficult to prove misappropriation under CUTSA because the Selling Shareholder could argue they did not believe the disclosed information was a trade secret, conflicting with the law's intent to protect clearly defined trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguity regarding what constitutes 'confidential information' by making it subjective ('what they reasonably consider'). This weakens the protection of trade secrets. The original text used the term 'shall not' which is a strong prohibition, however the changed text utilizes 'should attempt to not' weakening the obligation of the Selling Shareholder. The Law Explanation also mentions that this subjectivity makes it difficult to prove misappropriation under CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both refer to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The ambiguity introduced in the changed text is likely to make the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Selling Shareholder agrees to cooperate in any reasonable manner with any reasonable requests by Dollar Tree from time to time to verify compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, provided, however, Dollar Tree shall reimburse Selling Shareholder for Selling Shareholder's reasonable out-of-pocket costs in complying with such request.
perturbation.changed_text: Selling Shareholder may consider cooperating with requests by Dollar Tree from time to time to verify compliance with the provisions of this Agreement, and Dollar Tree might reimburse Selling Shareholder for what Dollar Tree considers reasonable costs.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause states Selling Shareholder 'agrees to cooperate' which indicates a commitment. Now it says 'may consider cooperating' which is not a commitment. Also, the initial text says 'Dollar Tree shall reimburse' which is mandatory, whereas the changed text uses 'might reimburse' which is not guaranteed. The phrase 'what Dollar Tree considers reasonable costs' leaves the reimbursement amount open to Dollar Tree's interpretation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 128.7
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly related to the cooperation clause itself, the changes to make it non-mandatory and vague regarding costs can impact the 'good faith' requirement implied in all contracts and litigation related to enforcing the contract. California Code of Civil Procedure Section 128.7 deals with frivolous lawsuits and sanctions for improper conduct in legal proceedings. The changes weaken the Selling Shareholder's duty to cooperate and creates grounds for disputes regarding reimbursement, potentially encouraging Dollar Tree to act without a good faith and objective belief the claim has merit if disputes arise regarding the modified cooperation agreement, given the ambiguity could encourage the company to take an unreasonable position related to the reimbursement.

Incidental Powers and Duties of Courts [128 - 130] ( Article 2 added by Code Amendments 1880, Ch. 35. ) 128.7. (a) Every pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper shall be signed by at least one attorney of record in the attorney’s individual name, or, if the party is not represented by an attorney, shall be signed by the party. Each paper shall state the signer’s address and telephone number, if any. Except when otherwise provided by law, pleadings need not be verified or accompanied by affidavit. An unsigned paper shall be stricken unless omission of the signature is corrected promptly after being called to the attention of the attorney or party. (b) By presenting to the court, whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating, a pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper, an attorney or unrepresented party is certi
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Code of Civil Procedure - CCP § 128.7 | FindLaw California Code, Code of Civil Procedure - CCP § 128.7 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff (a) Every pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper shall be signed by at least one attorney of record in the attorney's individual name, or, if the party is not represented by an attorney, shall be signed by the party. Each paper shall state the signer's address and telephone number, if any. Except when otherwise provided by law, pleadings need not be verified or accompanied by affidavit. An unsigned paper shall be stricken unless omission of the signature is corrected promptly after being called to the attention of the attorney or party. (b) By presenting to the court, whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating, a pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper, an attorney or unrepresented party is certifying that to the best of the person's knowledge, information, and belief, formed after an inquiry reasonable under the circumstances, all of the following conditions are met: (1) It is not being presented primarily for an improper purpose, such as to harass or to cause unnecessary delay or needless increase in the cost of litigation. (2) The claims, defenses, and other legal contentions therein are warranted by existing law or by a nonfrivolous argument for the extension, modification, or reversal of existing law or the establishment of new law. (3) The allegations and other factual contentions have evidentiary support or, if specifically so identified, are likely to have evidentiary support after a reasonable opportunity for further investigation or discovery. (4) The denials of factual contentions are warranted on the evidence or, if specifically so identified, are reasonably based on a lack of information or belief. (c) If, after notice and a reasonable opportunity to respond, the court determines that subdivision (b) has been violated, the court may, subject to the conditio
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the original obligations, creating a contradiction. The original text mandates cooperation and reimbursement ('agrees to cooperate', 'Dollar Tree shall reimburse'), while the changed text makes both discretionary ('may consider cooperating', 'might reimburse'). This shift introduces ambiguity and undermines the enforceability of the obligations. While the law snippets provided discuss frivolous lawsuits (CCP 128.7), the Law Explanation argues that weakening the duty to cooperate and creating vagueness regarding costs could encourage Dollar Tree to act without good faith if disputes arise. The changed text removes the guarantee of cooperation and reimbursement, fundamentally altering the legally binding nature of the original agreement, thus creating a contradiction under the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: From and after the execution of this Agreement, the Selling Shareholder shall not make any material public statement, representation or communication of any sort, whether oral, in writing, or by any other medium, which disparages Dollar Tree, its directors, officers, shareholders, partners, employees, products, services or financial condition.
perturbation.changed_text: From and after the execution of this Agreement, the Selling Shareholder should try to avoid making public statements that Dollar Tree might find disparaging.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not make any material public statement... which disparages' with 'should try to avoid making public statements that Dollar Tree might find disparaging' drastically weakens the clause. 'Should try to avoid' is not a strong prohibition, and 'Dollar Tree might find disparaging' makes the standard completely subjective and open to broad interpretation by Dollar Tree, introducing significant ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 96(k) prohibits employers from retaliating against employees who disclose information about the employer's working conditions. While this clause pertains to a Selling Shareholder and not necessarily an employee, the overly broad and subjective nature of the modified non-disparagement clause could be interpreted as a restriction on the Selling Shareholder's ability to speak freely about their past experiences with Dollar Tree, potentially chilling the expression of legitimate concerns that could relate to working conditions or other protected disclosures if the Selling Shareholder were a former employee. The law aims to prevent employers from silencing discussion about the employer's working conditions and related labor matters. The subjectivity creates the possibility that the Selling Shareholder's ability to discuss matters, especially past business operations, would be censored given what Dollar Tree might find disparaging.

DEPARTMENT OF INDUSTRIAL RELATIONS [50 - 182] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 4. Division of Labor Standards Enforcement [79 - 107] ( Heading of Chapter 4 amended by Stats. 1976, Ch. 746. ) 96. The Labor Commissioner and the deputies and representatives authorized by the commissioner in writing shall, upon the filing of a claim therefor by an employee, or an employee representative authorized in writing by an employee, with the Labor Commissioner, take assignments of: (a) Wage claims and incidental expense accounts and advances. (b) Mechanics’ and other liens of employees. (c) Claims based on “stop orders” for wages and on bonds for labor. (d) Claims for damages for misrepresentations of conditions of employment. (e) Claims for unreturned bond money of employees. (f) Claims for penalties for nonpayment of wages. (g) Claims for the return of workers’ tools in the illegal possession of another person. (h) Claims for vacation pay, severance pay, or other compensation supplemental to a wage agreement. (i) Awards for workers’ compensation benefits in which the Workers’ Compensation Appeals Board has found that the employer has failed to secure payment of compensation and where the award remains unpaid more than 10 days after having
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Code, Labor Code - LAB § 96 | FindLaw California Code, Labor Code - LAB § 96 Current as of January 01, 2023 | Updated by FindLaw Staff The Labor Commissioner and the deputies and representatives authorized by the commissioner in writing shall, upon the filing of a claim therefor by an employee, or an employee representative authorized in writing by an employee, with the Labor Commissioner, take assignments of: (a) Wage claims and incidental expense accounts and advances. (b) Mechanics' and other liens of employees. (c) Claims based on “stop orders” for wages and on bonds for labor. (d) Claims for damages for misrepresentations of conditions of employment. (e) Claims for unreturned bond money of employees. (f) Claims for penalties for nonpayment of wages. (g) Claims for the return of workers' tools in the illegal possession of another person. (h) Claims for vacation pay, severance pay, or other compensation supplemental to a wage agreement. (i) Awards for workers' compensation benefits in which the Workers' Compensation Appeals Board has found that the employer has failed to secure payment of compensation and where the award remains unpaid more than 10 days after having become final. (j) Claims for loss of wages as the result of discharge from employment for the garnishment of wages. (k) Claims for loss of wages as the result of demotion, suspension, or discharge from employment for lawful conduct occurring during nonworking hours away from the employer's premises. ( l ) Claims for violations of standards issued by the Fast Food Council pursuant to Part 4.5.5 (commencing with Section 1470 ) of Division 2. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces significant ambiguity, weakening the non-disparagement clause. The original text's 'shall not make any material public statement... which disparages' is replaced with the far weaker 'should try to avoid making public statements that Dollar Tree might find disparaging.' This introduces ambiguity because 'should try to avoid' is not a strict prohibition, and 'Dollar Tree might find disparaging' makes the standard subjective and open to broad interpretation. While the snippets do not directly address non-disparagement clauses, the law explanation describes how California Labor Code Section 96(k) aims to prevent employers from silencing discussions about working conditions. The altered clause's subjectivity potentially allows Dollar Tree to censor a Selling Shareholder's discussion of past business operations if Dollar Tree subjectively finds it disparaging. Therefore, by creating ambiguity, the contract weakens protections for speech that could potentially be considered protected disclosures.
